advantages: Provides a unified language for reasoning about many data types (features, embeddings, signals) using the same linear algebra tools.
Enables powerful geometric intuition: distances, angles, projections and subspaces all become concrete and visually interpretable.
Separates coordinates from geometry, so you can change bases without changing the underlying vectors, which simplifies many derivations.
Aligns directly with how ML libraries and hardware work, since GPUs and linear algebra kernels are optimized for vector and matrix operations.
disadvantages: The abstraction can feel heavy at first, with many definitions (span, basis, dimension, subspace) that are easy to confuse.
Thinking purely in vector-space terms can hide numerical issues such as conditioning and floating-point error that matter in implementations.
Not every structure of interest is exactly a vector space (for example, probability simplices and many manifolds), so some intuition must be adapted.
High-dimensional vector spaces quickly exceed human visual intuition, so geometric pictures from R^2 and R^3 only partially transfer.
